text: A sixty-one-year-old woman was referred to our department, and colonoscopy revealed an ulcerofungating mass at the cecum. Abdominal computed tomography and positron emission tomography revealed a cecal mass with enlarged lymph nodes in the pericolic and ileocolic areas.
A schematic view shows the access port setup for robotic right hemicolectomy, with laparoscopic submucosal injection of indocyanine green around the tumor at the start of the operation.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum at the retrocecal recess and performs inferior to superior mobilization of the ascending colon. An avascular surgical plane along Told's line is exposed, revealing the duodenum and right kidney, with dissection continuing up to the subhepatic area.

Following lateral mobilization, the surgeon performs primary vascular control with D-III lymphadenectomy along the right border of the superior mesenteric vessels. Intraoperative near-infrared fluorescence imaging is used for lymphatic mapping, and lymph node dissection is conducted along the central vascular trunk despite the inability to identify lymphatic flow visually.

Intravenous injection of five milligrams of endocyanine green dye is administered, followed by near-infrared fluorescence imaging that reveals the vascular anatomy near the ileocolic vessels. The ileocolic artery is visualized first, followed by the ileocolic vein, approximately ten seconds after the dye injection.

The surgeon ligates the ileocolic vein with hemo-lock clips and transects it at its root, followed by ligation and transection of the ileocolic artery. Near-infrared fluorescence imaging is used to identify remnant lymph nodes around the ligated ileocolic vessels, and suspicious lymph node tissue is dissected for optimized lymph node removal.

The surgeon transects the greater omentum of the transverse colon and detaches the attachment of the hepatic flexure. The transverse mesocolon is divided using the endo-wrist-I vessel sealer to prepare for anastomosis. Intravenous injection of ICG dye is performed prior to near-infrared fluorescence imaging to assess bowel perfusion, allowing the surgeon to choose the optimal point of division.

The surgeon transects the transverse colon using the endo-wrist stapler, then introduces the stapler to create a side-to-side anastomosis in an isoparastaltic manner. The monopolar curved scissors are replaced with a suture-cut needle driver, and the stapler insertion site is closed with continuous stitches using V-lock sutures.
